Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty inconsistent throughout the course. I'd try to set aside time on weekends since I'm juggling four online courses at once, but with limited resources at home, I often had to work around when I could access good internet. I found myself reading through materials late at night when things were quieter, taking notes by hand since that's how I learn best. Some weeks I'd manage maybe 17 hours total across everything, but it wasn't evenly distributed.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. I wish I could say I had a consistent schedule, but between all my courses and life stuff, I'd often end up cramming when deadlines approached. There were weeks where I barely touched the course, and then suddenly I'd be intensely focused for a few days. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I managed to keep my head above water.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or wanting to check if new materials were posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to download readings so I could study them offline later since my internet isn't always reliable. Discussion posts also prompted me to log in because I actually enjoyed the peer interactions when I had time. Honestly, if there wasn't something specific pulling me in, I might go days without checking.
Q4: Not really a consistent routine, no. There were brief periods where I'd check in every couple of days, especially early in the semester when I was more motivated. But it never became automatic for me like checking my phone or email. The platform felt more like a tool I used when needed rather than something integrated into my daily life.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were definitely most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I appreciated when instructors posted clear text summaries alongside videos because I could engage with those more deeply. The discussion forums were also valuable when peers shared different perspectives on the readings. I'd often copy key points into my own notes and review those instead of rewatching videos.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped or minimized the video content. With over 160 minutes of video to watch, I'd sometimes just skim through or watch at double speed to get the main points. My internet connection made streaming frustrating, and honestly, I retain information better when I read it anyway. Some of the supplementary resources felt redundant, so I'd skip those to focus on what seemed essential.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to assignments or exams. If something helped me understand concepts I'd need for my work, I was much more willing to invest in it. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers and get different viewpoints. When something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grades, my motivation dropped significantly.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were the first things I'd cut when time got tight. Required activities had this weight to them because I knew they'd affect my grade, so I'd prioritize those even when I was exhausted. Optional stuff, even if interesting, felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford given everything else on my plate. I sometimes felt guilty about skipping them, but I had to be strategic with my limited time and energy.
Q9: Oh absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then study from printed notes or handwritten summaries. I couldn't always rely on having stable internet, so I learned to prepare materials in advance. I also used some free textbook resources I found online and would cross-reference those with course content. A lot of my learning happened with just my notebook and a cup of tea.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes, rewriting concepts in my own words, or working through problems on paper. Sometimes I'd discuss course topics with a friend over the phone, which really helped solidify my understanding. My exam scores came from this kind of offline preparation, not from time logged on the platform.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd log in, download everything I needed, and then work through it on my own time without being connected. One login might result in several days of studying from those materials. It was more practical given my internet situation and honestly helped me focus better without the distractions of being online. My assignment scores reflect this work even if my login frequency doesn't.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when assignments from all four courses seemed to pile up at once. My motivation dipped pretty low during those times, and I'd feel this moderate anxiety building up. I don't have a lot of resilience when things get overwhelming—I tend to shut down a bit before I can push through. The CBT sessions I attended actually helped me work through some of those harder moments.
Q13: Absolutely, all of those factors played a role. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS—just enough to not fall completely behind. Confusion about course expectations sometimes made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face what I didn't understand. Competing responsibilities, especially with limited resources, meant the LMS often got pushed aside for more immediate concerns.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I might technically be logged in and clicking through pages, but I wasn't absorbing anything meaningful. This usually happened when I was tired or stressed about other things. I'd have to go back later and actually engage with the content properly, which the system wouldn't really capture.
Q15: I definitely procrastinated on things that felt overwhelming or confusing, even when I knew they mattered. Sometimes the anxiety of not understanding something made me avoid it rather than face it head-on. I'd tell myself I'd get to it tomorrow, and then suddenly a week had passed. The CBT sessions helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was still a struggle throughout the course.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active—downloading materials, posting in discussions, submitting work. Without that external pressure, my engagement would drift. It wasn't the healthiest pattern, but it's how I managed to complete enough assignments to pass. My activity probably looked very spiky because of this.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, though not always positively. When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me to keep going and engage more with similar content. But when feedback was delayed or unclear, I'd feel disconnected and unsure if I was on the right track. I appreciated when instructors acknowledged my written work specifically since that's where I put most of my effort.
Q18: The course structure had a big impact on how I participated. When modules were clearly organized with explicit expectations, I could plan my offline studying better. But when things felt scattered or the path forward wasn't clear, I'd disengage more easily. Having four online courses meant I gravitated toward whichever one had the clearest structure at any given time.
Q19: The availability of downloadable text resources made engagement easier for me since I could study them offline. However, when too much content was video-based or required constant internet connection, that created barriers. The discussion forums were well-designed and made me feel connected to peers, which helped. What made it harder was when deadlines weren't clearly visible or when navigation felt confusing.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are trying to figure things out but struggling to produce finished work. Maybe they're reading everything, watching videos, participating in discussions, but then hit a wall when it comes to actually completing assignments. It could be anxiety, confusion about expectations, or just not having the skills to translate understanding into submitted work. Activity doesn't always equal productive output.
Q21: That actually describes my situation pretty well. My exam score and assignment scores were decent even though my LMS activity was relatively low. I did most of my real learning offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. The LMS was just a gateway to get resources, not where my actual studying happened. Someone looking at my login frequency alone would probably think I wasn't engaged, but my grades tell a different story.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and how often they logged in. They might capture participation in discussions or time spent on certain pages. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement, emotional state, or whether someone actually understood what they were looking at. They're like seeing footprints without knowing anything about the journey.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, making connections, writing things in my own words, and feeling curious about learning more. It's not about how many times I click on something—it's about whether my mind is genuinely invested in making sense of the content. Some of my most engaged moments happened with just a notebook and no screen in sight.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed readings at my kitchen table, or the conversations with friends that helped clarify difficult concepts. It wouldn't capture the anxiety I worked through with CBT sessions, or how happy I felt when things finally clicked after struggling. It misses the resourcefulness required when you have limited resources but still want to learn. My engagement was real and meaningful—it just mostly happened offline.
